title: The Rain on the Window
text_zh: 下雨的时候我喜欢关掉灯，坐在窗边看雨滴在玻璃上滑下来。每一滴雨都有自己的路线——有的直直地往下走，有的左右摇摆，有的会停在中间好久，突然和另一滴合并，变成一滴大雨珠飞快地冲到底部。小时候我把这当成赛跑：选两滴雨，看哪一滴先到达窗户的下边缘。现在长大了还是会不自觉地这么做。窗外的世界在雨水的扭曲下变得模糊，路灯变成一团团黄色的光晕，行人变成移动的影子。雨声是一种很好的白噪音，不像音乐那样需要注意力，只是存在着，填满了房间里每一个安静的角落。我发现下雨天让人更容易思考——也许因为整个世界都慢了下来，连时间都好像变得不那么赶了。
text_pinyin: Xià yǔ de shíhou wǒ xǐhuan guān diào dēng, zuò zài chuāng biān kàn yǔdī zài bōli shàng huá xiàlái. Měi yì dī yǔ dōu yǒu zìjǐ de lùxiàn — yǒu de zhí zhí de wǎng xià zǒu, yǒu de zuǒyòu yáobǎi, yǒu de huì tíng zài zhōngjiān hǎo jiǔ, tūrán hé lìng yì dī hébìng, biàn chéng yì dī dà yǔzhū fēikuài de chōng dào dǐbù. Xiǎo shíhou wǒ bǎ zhè dàng chéng sàipǎo: xuǎn liǎng dī yǔ, kàn nǎ yì dī xiān dàodá chuānghù de xià biānyuán. Xiànzài zhǎng dà le háishi huì bù zìjué de zhème zuò. Chuāng wài de shìjiè zài yǔshuǐ de niǔqū xià biàn de móhu, lùdēng biàn chéng yì tuántuán huángsè de guāngyūn, xíngrén biàn chéng yídòng de yǐngzi. Yǔ shēng shì yì zhǒng hěn hǎo de bái zàoyīn, bú xiàng yīnyuè nàyàng xūyào zhùyìlì, zhǐ shì cúnzài zhe, tián mǎn le fángjiān lǐ měi yí gè ānjìng de jiǎoluò. Wǒ fāxiàn xià yǔ tiān ràng rén gèng róngyì sīkǎo — yěxǔ yīnwèi zhěnggè shìjiè dōu màn le xiàlái, lián shíjiān dōu hǎoxiàng biàn de bú nàme gǎn le.
text_en: When it rains I like to turn off the lights and sit by the window watching raindrops slide down the glass. Each raindrop has its own path — some go straight down, some sway left and right, some pause in the middle for a long time then suddenly merge with another drop, becoming a large bead that races to the bottom. As a child I treated this as a race: pick two drops and see which reaches the bottom edge first. Now grown up, I still do it without thinking. The world outside becomes blurry through the distortion of rain — streetlights become clusters of yellow halos, pedestrians become moving shadows. The sound of rain is wonderful white noise, not like music that demands attention, just existing, filling every quiet corner of the room. I've found that rainy days make it easier to think — perhaps because the whole world has slowed down, and even time seems less rushed.

questions.q_zh: 作者小时候把雨滴当成什么？
questions.q_en: What did the author treat raindrops as when young?

questions.q_zh: 作者认为雨声和音乐有什么不同？
questions.q_en: How does the author think rain sounds differ from music?

options.text: 雨声不需要注意力，只是存在着
options.pinyin: yǔ shēng bù xūyào zhùyìlì, zhǐ shì cúnzài zhe
options.text_en: Rain doesn't demand attention, just exists

questions.q_zh: 为什么作者觉得下雨天更容易思考？
questions.q_en: Why does the author think rainy days are better for thinking?

options.text: 因为整个世界都慢了下来
options.pinyin: yīnwèi zhěnggè shìjiè dōu màn le xiàlái
options.text_en: Because the whole world has slowed down

title: The Overnight Train
text_zh: 我坐过一次从北京到成都的夜间火车，硬卧，下铺。晚上九点上车，第二天下午一点到。火车刚开动的时候，车厢里很热闹：有人泡方便面，有人打牌，小孩子在过道里跑来跑去。但是到了十点左右，列车员过来把大灯关了，只留下过道的小灯，整个车厢突然安静下来。我躺在下铺，能听到火车有节奏的声音：咔嗒咔嗒，咔嗒咔嗒。窗帘没有完全拉上，偶尔能看到外面经过的小站——站台上几乎没有人，只有一盏昏暗的灯。我不知道那是什么地方，大概是某个我永远不会去的小城市。对面上铺的人已经开始打呼噜了，声音很稳定，让人莫名其妙地安心。在火车上睡觉有一种独特的感觉：身体在移动，但意识在休息。你醒来的时候已经到了另一个城市，像是被时间偷偷搬运了一段距离。
text_pinyin: Wǒ zuò guò yí cì cóng Běijīng dào Chéngdū de yèjiān huǒchē, yìngwò, xiàpù. Wǎnshang jiǔ diǎn shàng chē, dì èr tiān xiàwǔ yì diǎn dào. Huǒchē gāng kāidòng de shíhou, chēxiāng lǐ hěn rènao: yǒu rén pào fāngbiàn miàn, yǒu rén dǎpái, xiǎo háizi zài guòdào lǐ pǎo lái pǎo qù. Dànshì dào le shí diǎn zuǒyòu, lièchēyuán guòlái bǎ dà dēng guān le, zhǐ liú xià guòdào de xiǎo dēng, zhěnggè chēxiāng tūrán ānjìng xiàlái. Wǒ tǎng zài xiàpù, néng tīng dào huǒchē yǒu jiézòu de shēngyīn: kādā kādā, kādā kādā. Chuānglián méiyǒu wánquán lā shàng, ǒu'ěr néng kàn dào wàimiàn jīngguò de xiǎo zhàn — zhàntái shàng jīhū méiyǒu rén, zhǐ yǒu yì zhǎn hūn'àn de dēng. Wǒ bù zhīdào nà shì shénme dìfāng, dàgài shì mǒu gè wǒ yǒngyuǎn bú huì qù de xiǎo chéngshì. Duìmiàn shàngpù de rén yǐjīng kāishǐ dǎ hūlu le, shēngyīn hěn wěndìng, ràng rén mòmíngqímiào de ānxīn. Zài huǒchē shàng shuìjiào yǒu yì zhǒng dútè de gǎnjué: shēntǐ zài yídòng, dàn yìshí zài xiūxi. Nǐ xǐng lái de shíhou yǐjīng dào le lìng yí gè chéngshì, xiàng shì bèi shíjiān tōutōu bānyùn le yí duàn jùlí.
text_en: I once took an overnight train from Beijing to Chengdu — hard sleeper, lower bunk. Boarded at nine PM, arrived the next afternoon at one. When the train first started the carriage was lively: people making instant noodles, playing cards, kids running up and down the aisle. But around ten the conductor came and turned off the main lights, leaving only the small aisle lights, and the whole carriage suddenly fell quiet. Lying on my lower bunk I could hear the train's rhythmic sound: clickety-clack, clickety-clack. The curtain wasn't fully drawn and I could occasionally see small stations passing outside — nearly empty platforms with just a dim light. I didn't know where they were, probably some small city I'd never visit. The person on the upper bunk across from me had started snoring steadily, which was inexplicably reassuring. Sleeping on a train has a unique feeling: your body is moving but your consciousness is resting. When you wake up you're in another city, as if time secretly transported you across a distance.

questions.q_zh: 火车什么时候安静下来的？
questions.q_en: When did the train car become quiet?

options.text: 十点左右列车员关了大灯以后
options.pinyin: shí diǎn zuǒyòu lièchēyuán guān le dà dēng yǐhòu
options.text_en: Around ten after the conductor turned off the lights

questions.q_zh: 作者看到的小站是什么样的？
questions.q_en: What were the small stations like?

options.text: 几乎没有人，只有一盏昏暗的灯
options.pinyin: jīhū méiyǒu rén, zhǐ yǒu yì zhǎn hūn'àn de dēng
options.text_en: Nearly empty with just a dim light

options.pinyin: zhàntái shàng yǒu hěn duō gōngrén

questions.q_zh: 作者认为在火车上睡觉的感觉是什么？
questions.q_en: How does the author describe sleeping on a train?

options.text: 身体在移动，意识在休息
options.pinyin: shēntǐ zài yídòng, yìshí zài xiūxi

title: Why Old Neighborhoods Feel Warmer
text_zh: 我在一个新建的高档小区住了两年以后，搬到了一个二十年前盖的老小区。朋友们都很惊讶，问我为什么要「降级」。但是住进来以后我才知道什么叫做真正的邻居关系。在新小区，我住了两年不知道隔壁住着谁。电梯里碰到邻居，大家都低着头看手机，最多点一下头。在老小区完全不一样。搬进来第一天，隔壁的阿姨就端着一盘饺子过来说「先吃着，搬家忙，别自己做了」。楼下的大爷每天傍晚在院子里下棋，谁路过都可以看一局。快递员知道每一户人家什么时候在家。我觉得新小区的问题不是设计不好，而是设计得太好了——每一户都是独立的、隔音的、不需要和任何人发生关系的。老小区虽然墙薄、走廊窄、停车位不够，但正是这些「不方便」创造了人和人之间交流的机会。
text_pinyin: Wǒ zài yí gè xīn jiàn de gāodǎng xiǎoqū zhù le liǎng nián yǐhòu, bān dào le yí gè èrshí nián qián gài de lǎo xiǎoqū. Péngyoumen dōu hěn jīngyà, wèn wǒ wèi shénme yào「jiàngjí」. Dànshì zhù jìnlái yǐhòu wǒ cái zhīdào shénme jiào zuò zhēnzhèng de línjū guānxi. Zài xīn xiǎoqū, wǒ zhù le liǎng nián bù zhīdào gébì zhù zhe shéi. Diàntī lǐ pèng dào línjū, dàjiā dōu dī zhe tóu kàn shǒujī, zuì duō diǎn yíxià tóu. Zài lǎo xiǎoqū wánquán bù yíyàng. Bān jìnlái dì yī tiān, gébì de āyí jiù duān zhe yì pán jiǎozi guòlái shuō「xiān chī zhe, bānjiā máng, bié zìjǐ zuò le」. Lóu xià de dàyé měi tiān bàngwǎn zài yuànzi lǐ xià qí, shéi lùguò dōu kěyǐ kàn yí jú. Kuàidìyuán zhīdào měi yí hù rénjiā shénme shíhou zài jiā. Wǒ juéde xīn xiǎoqū de wèntí bú shì shèjì bù hǎo, ér shì shèjì de tài hǎo le — měi yí hù dōu shì dúlì de, géyīn de, bù xūyào hé rènhé rén fāshēng guānxi de. Lǎo xiǎoqū suīrán qiáng báo, zǒuláng zhǎi, tíngchē wèi bú gòu, dàn zhèng shì zhèxiē「bù fāngbiàn」chuàngzào le rén hé rén zhī jiān jiāoliú de jīhuì.
text_en: After living two years in a newly built upscale compound, I moved to an old compound built twenty years ago. Friends were all surprised, asking why I wanted to 'downgrade.' But only after moving in did I learn what real neighbor relationships are. In the new compound, I lived two years without knowing who lived next door. Meeting neighbors in the elevator, everyone looked down at their phones, at most giving a nod. The old compound is completely different. On my first day moving in, the auntie next door came over with a plate of dumplings saying 'eat these first, you're busy moving, don't cook yourself.' The grandpa downstairs plays chess in the courtyard every evening and anyone passing by can watch a game. The delivery workers know when each household is home. I think the problem with new compounds isn't bad design but too-good design — every unit is independent, soundproof, requiring no connection with anyone. The old compound has thin walls, narrow corridors, and not enough parking, but it's precisely these 'inconveniences' that create opportunities for people to interact.

questions.q_zh: 搬进来第一天邻居做了什么？
questions.q_en: What did the neighbor do on the first day?

options.pinyin: duān le yì pán jiǎozi guòlái
options.text_en: Brought over a plate of dumplings

questions.q_zh: 作者认为新小区的问题是什么？
questions.q_en: What does the author think is the problem with new compounds?

options.text: 设计得太好了，不需要和别人交流
options.pinyin: shèjì de tài hǎo le, bù xūyào hé biérén jiāoliú
options.text_en: Designed too well, no need to interact with others

questions.q_zh: 什么创造了人和人之间交流的机会？
questions.q_en: What creates opportunities for interaction between people?

title: The Fruit Stand's Seasons
text_zh: 小区门口的水果摊是我的日历。春天的时候，摊上开始出现草莓和樱桃，颜色鲜艳，价格也贵。老板会说「刚上市的，尝尝」。到了夏天，西瓜堆成了小山，整个摊位都散发着甜味。秋天是水果最丰富的季节——苹果、梨、柿子、石榴、橘子，摊位不够大了，地上也摆满了箱子。冬天的种类最少，主要是橘子和苹果，偶尔有进口的车厘子，贵得让人犹豫。老板很会做生意，他从来不催你买，总是先让你尝一口。他切水果的刀法很快，一个苹果三秒钟就变成八瓣。我在这个摊位买了五年的水果，看着老板的女儿从小学生变成了初中生。她放学以后会在摊位旁边写作业，老板偶尔低头看她一眼。水果在变，季节在转，但那个父女俩守着的小摊位一直在那里，像这条街上最稳定的存在。
text_pinyin: Xiǎoqū ménkǒu de shuǐguǒ tān shì wǒ de rìlì. Chūntiān de shíhou, tān shàng kāishǐ chūxiàn cǎoméi hé yīngtáo, yánsè xiānyàn, jiàgé yě guì. Lǎobǎn huì shuō「gāng shàngshì de, cháng cháng」. Dào le xiàtiān, xīguā duī chéng le xiǎoshān, zhěnggè tānwèi dōu sànfā zhe tiánwèi. Qiūtiān shì shuǐguǒ zuì fēngfù de jìjié — píngguǒ, lí, shìzi, shíliu, júzi, tānwèi bú gòu dà le, dìshàng yě bǎi mǎn le xiāngzi. Dōngtiān de zhǒnglèi zuì shǎo, zhǔyào shì júzi hé píngguǒ, ǒu'ěr yǒu jìnkǒu de chēlízi, guì de ràng rén yóuyù. Lǎobǎn hěn huì zuò shēngyi, tā cónglái bù cuī nǐ mǎi, zǒng shì xiān ràng nǐ cháng yì kǒu. Tā qiē shuǐguǒ de dāofǎ hěn kuài, yí gè píngguǒ sān miǎozhōng jiù biàn chéng bā bàn. Wǒ zài zhège tānwèi mǎi le wǔ nián de shuǐguǒ, kàn zhe lǎobǎn de nǚ'ér cóng xiǎoxuéshēng biàn chéng le chūzhōngshēng. Tā fàngxué yǐhòu huì zài tānwèi pángbiān xiě zuòyè, lǎobǎn ǒu'ěr dītóu kàn tā yì yǎn. Shuǐguǒ zài biàn, jìjié zài zhuǎn, dàn nàge fùnǚ liǎ shǒu zhe de xiǎo tānwèi yìzhí zài nàlǐ, xiàng zhè tiáo jiē shàng zuì wěndìng de cúnzài.
text_en: The fruit stand at the compound entrance is my calendar. In spring, strawberries and cherries appear — bright colors, high prices. The owner says 'just came to market, try some.' In summer, watermelons pile up like small hills and the whole stand smells sweet. Autumn is the richest season for fruit — apples, pears, persimmons, pomegranates, oranges — the stand isn't big enough and boxes fill the ground too. Winter has the fewest varieties, mainly oranges and apples, occasionally imported cherries so expensive they make you hesitate. The owner is good at business — he never rushes you to buy, always letting you taste first. His knife work cutting fruit is fast; an apple becomes eight slices in three seconds. I've bought fruit at this stand for five years, watching his daughter grow from an elementary student to a middle schooler. After school she does homework beside the stand, and the owner occasionally glances down at her. The fruit changes, the seasons turn, but the little stand this father and daughter tend has always been there — like the most stable presence on this street.

questions.q_zh: 哪个季节水果种类最多？
questions.q_en: Which season has the most varieties of fruit?

questions.q_zh: 老板的女儿放学后做什么？
questions.q_en: What does the owner's daughter do after school?

questions.q_zh: 作者觉得水果摊像什么？
questions.q_en: What does the author think the fruit stand is like?

options.text: 这条街上最稳定的存在
options.pinyin: zhè tiáo jiē shàng zuì wěndìng de cúnzài
options.text_en: The most stable presence on the street

title: The Sound of Chopping Vegetables
text_zh: 傍晚六点左右，如果你打开窗户，就能听到整栋楼传来切菜的声音。嗒嗒嗒，嗒嗒嗒——每家的节奏都不一样。楼下那家切得又快又均匀，像是一个厨艺很好的人。隔壁那家比较慢，中间还会停下来，也许在看手机上的菜谱。有一家的声音特别重，刀剁在砧板上像打鼓一样，那应该是在剁肉馅。这些声音混在一起，加上油锅的滋滋声和偶尔的抽油烟机轰鸣，组成了一首属于傍晚的交响乐。我妈妈切菜的声音我一听就能认出来——不快不慢，很有节奏感，中间没有犹豫。她说这是做了三十年饭练出来的。我自己切菜的声音就差多了，有时候快有时候慢，还会不小心停下来检查切得均不均匀。也许再过三十年，我的切菜声也会变成别人窗口飘进来的安心的背景音。
text_pinyin: Bàngwǎn liù diǎn zuǒyòu, rúguǒ nǐ dǎkāi chuānghu, jiù néng tīng dào zhěng dòng lóu chuán lái qiē cài de shēngyīn. Dādādā, dādādā — měi jiā de jiézòu dōu bù yíyàng. Lóu xià nà jiā qiē de yòu kuài yòu jūnyún, xiàng shì yí gè chúyì hěn hǎo de rén. Gébì nà jiā bǐjiào màn, zhōngjiān hái huì tíng xiàlái, yěxǔ zài kàn shǒujī shàng de càipǔ. Yǒu yì jiā de shēngyīn tèbié zhòng, dāo duò zài zhēnbǎn shàng xiàng dǎ gǔ yíyàng, nà yīnggāi shì zài duò ròuxiàn. Zhèxiē shēngyīn hùn zài yìqǐ, jiā shàng yóu guō de zīzī shēng hé ǒu'ěr de chōu yóuyān jī hōngmíng, zǔchéng le yì shǒu shǔyú bàngwǎn de jiāoxiǎngyuè. Wǒ māma qiē cài de shēngyīn wǒ yì tīng jiù néng rèn chūlái — bú kuài bú màn, hěn yǒu jiézòugǎn, zhōngjiān méiyǒu yóuyù. Tā shuō zhè shì zuò le sānshí nián fàn liàn chūlái de. Wǒ zìjǐ qiē cài de shēngyīn jiù chà duō le, yǒu shíhou kuài yǒu shíhou màn, hái huì bù xiǎoxīn tíng xiàlái jiǎnchá qiē de jūn bù jūnyún. Yěxǔ zài guò sānshí nián, wǒ de qiē cài shēng yě huì biàn chéng biérén chuāngkǒu piāo jìnlái de ānxīn de bèijǐng yīn.
text_en: Around six in the evening, if you open the window, you can hear the sound of chopping vegetables from the whole building. Tap-tap-tap, tap-tap-tap — every household has a different rhythm. The one downstairs chops fast and evenly, like someone with good cooking skills. The one next door is slower, pausing in the middle — maybe checking a recipe on their phone. One household chops very heavily, the knife hitting the cutting board like a drum — that's probably mincing meat. These sounds blend together with the sizzle of oil pans and the occasional roar of range hoods, composing an evening symphony. I can recognize my mother's chopping sound instantly — neither fast nor slow, very rhythmic, no hesitation in between. She says that comes from thirty years of cooking. My own chopping sounds are much worse — sometimes fast, sometimes slow, occasionally stopping to check if the cuts are even. Maybe in another thirty years, my chopping sound will become the reassuring background noise drifting into someone else's window.

questions.q_zh: 声音特别重的那家可能在做什么？
questions.q_en: What is the household with the heaviest chopping sound probably doing?

questions.q_zh: 妈妈的切菜声音有什么特点？
questions.q_en: What's special about the mother's chopping sound?

options.pinyin: bú kuài bú màn, hěn yǒu jiézòugǎn
options.text_en: Neither fast nor slow, very rhythmic

questions.q_en: What does the author hope their chopping sound becomes in the future?

options.text: 别人窗口飘进来的安心的背景音

title: The Post Office Counter
text_zh: 我去邮局寄一个包裹给外地的朋友。现在大家都用快递，邮局的人很少。只有两个柜台开着，一个办理汇款，一个办理包裹。帮我办理的是一个中年女人，动作不快但非常仔细。她让我在包裹单上写收件人的地址和电话，我写完以后她又检查了一遍，发现我的邮编少写了一位数，让我补上。然后她称了重量，告诉我价格，问我要不要保价。整个过程不到十分钟，但每一步都有固定的流程。我注意到柜台后面的墙上贴着很多通知，最显眼的一张写着「请核对好地址再寄出」。旁边还贴着一张已经发黄的宣传画，上面写着「人民邮政为人民」。在快递公司到处都是的今天，邮局就像一个慢下来的时空：排队的人不着急，工作人员不慌张，包裹会按照它该走的速度到达。也许不是所有事情都需要那么快。
text_pinyin: Wǒ qù yóujú jì yí gè bāoguǒ gěi wàidì de péngyou. Xiànzài dàjiā dōu yòng kuàidì, yóujú de rén hěn shǎo. Zhǐ yǒu liǎng gè guìtái kāi zhe, yí gè bànlǐ huìkuǎn, yí gè bànlǐ bāoguǒ. Bāng wǒ bànlǐ de shì yí gè zhōngnián nǚrén, dòngzuò bú kuài dàn fēicháng zǐxì. Tā ràng wǒ zài bāoguǒ dān shàng xiě shōujiàn rén de dìzhǐ hé diànhuà, wǒ xiě wán yǐhòu tā yòu jiǎnchá le yí biàn, fāxiàn wǒ de yóubiān shǎo xiě le yí wèi shù, ràng wǒ bǔ shàng. Ránhòu tā chēng le zhòngliàng, gàosu wǒ jiàgé, wèn wǒ yào bu yào bǎojià. Zhěnggè guòchéng bú dào shí fēnzhōng, dàn měi yí bù dōu yǒu gùdìng de liúchéng. Wǒ zhùyì dào guìtái hòumiàn de qiáng shàng tiē zhe hěn duō tōngzhī, zuì xiǎnyǎn de yì zhāng xiě zhe「qǐng héduì hǎo dìzhǐ zài jì chū」. Pángbiān hái tiē zhe yì zhāng yǐjīng fā huáng de xuānchuán huà, shàngmiàn xiě zhe「rénmín yóuzhèng wèi rénmín」. Zài kuàidì gōngsī dàochù dōu shì de jīntiān, yóujú jiù xiàng yí gè màn xiàlái de shíkōng: páiduì de rén bù zháojí, gōngzuò rényuán bù huāngzhāng, bāoguǒ huì ànzhào tā gāi zǒu de sùdù dàodá. Yěxǔ bú shì suǒyǒu shìqing dōu xūyào nàme kuài.
text_en: I went to the post office to mail a package to a friend in another city. Now that everyone uses express delivery, the post office has few people. Only two counters were open — one for money transfers, one for packages. The woman helping me was middle-aged, not fast but very careful. She had me write the recipient's address and phone number on the parcel form. After I finished she checked it again, found I'd left out one digit of the postal code, and had me add it. Then she weighed it, told me the price, and asked if I wanted insurance. The whole process took less than ten minutes, but every step had a fixed procedure. I noticed many notices on the wall behind the counter. The most prominent one read 'please verify the address before mailing.' Next to it was a yellowed propaganda poster reading 'People's Post Office Serves the People.' In a world full of express delivery companies today, the post office is like a pocket of slowed-down space-time: the people in line aren't hurried, the staff aren't flustered, and packages arrive at the speed they're meant to. Maybe not everything needs to be so fast.

questions.q_zh: 作者的包裹单有什么问题？
questions.q_en: What was wrong with the author's parcel form?

options.text_en: One digit missing from the postal code

questions.q_en: What does the yellowed poster say?

options.text_en: People's Post Office Serves the People

options.pinyin: yí gè màn xiàlái de shíkōng

title: The Cat on the Wall
text_zh: 我们小区有一只橘色的猫，没有人知道它是谁家的，也许它不属于任何人。它最喜欢的地方是小区围墙上面的一段——那里刚好有一棵树的树枝伸过来，形成了一小片阴凉。每天下午两三点钟它都会出现在那里，有时候趴着，有时候坐着，尾巴懒洋洋地垂下来。它从墙上往下看小区里发生的一切：老人散步、小孩玩耍、狗被主人牵着走过。它从来不下来参加这些活动，只是观察。有时候有人抬头看到它，会说「又在那儿了」，语气里有一种熟悉的温暖。下雨天它会消失，但第二天太阳出来以后又准时回到墙上。有一次我走到墙下面想摸它，它低下头看了我一眼，眼神不害怕也不亲热，只是一种平静的打量——好像在说「我知道你在这里住，你也知道我在这里住，这就够了」。
text_pinyin: Wǒmen xiǎoqū yǒu yì zhī júsè de māo, méiyǒu rén zhīdào tā shì shéi jiā de, yěxǔ tā bù shǔyú rènhé rén. Tā zuì xǐhuan de dìfāng shì xiǎoqū wéiqiáng shàngmiàn de yí duàn — nàlǐ gānghǎo yǒu yì kē shù de shùzhī shēn guòlái, xíngchéng le yì xiǎo piàn yīnliáng. Měi tiān xiàwǔ liǎng sān diǎnzhōng tā dōu huì chūxiàn zài nàlǐ, yǒu shíhou pā zhe, yǒu shíhou zuò zhe, wěiba lǎnyāngyāng de chuí xiàlái. Tā cóng qiáng shàng wǎng xià kàn xiǎoqū lǐ fāshēng de yíqiè: lǎorén sànbù, xiǎohái wánshuǎ, gǒu bèi zhǔrén qiān zhe zǒu guò. Tā cónglái bù xiàlái cānjiā zhèxiē huódòng, zhǐ shì guānchá. Yǒu shíhou yǒu rén táitóu kàn dào tā, huì shuō「yòu zài nàr le」, yǔqì lǐ yǒu yì zhǒng shúxī de wēnnuǎn. Xià yǔ tiān tā huì xiāoshī, dàn dì èr tiān tàiyáng chūlái yǐhòu yòu zhǔnshí huí dào qiáng shàng. Yǒu yí cì wǒ zǒu dào qiáng xiàmiàn xiǎng mō tā, tā dīxià tóu kàn le wǒ yì yǎn, yǎnshén bú hàipà yě bù qīnrè, zhǐ shì yì zhǒng píngjìng de dǎliang — hǎoxiàng zài shuō「wǒ zhīdào nǐ zài zhèlǐ zhù, nǐ yě zhīdào wǒ zài zhèlǐ zhù, zhè jiù gòu le」.
text_en: Our compound has an orange cat. Nobody knows whose it is — maybe it belongs to no one. Its favorite spot is a section of the compound wall where a tree branch extends over, creating a small patch of shade. Every afternoon around two or three it appears there, sometimes lying down, sometimes sitting, tail dangling lazily. From the wall it watches everything happening below: old people strolling, children playing, dogs being walked past on leashes. It never comes down to join these activities, only observes. Sometimes someone looks up and sees it and says 'there it is again,' with a warm familiarity in their voice. On rainy days it disappears, but the next day when the sun comes out it returns to the wall right on time. Once I walked beneath the wall wanting to pet it. It looked down at me — not afraid, not affectionate, just a calm appraisal — as if to say 'I know you live here, you know I live here, that's enough.'

questions.q_zh: 猫最喜欢的地方有什么特别？
questions.q_en: What's special about the cat's favorite spot?

options.text_en: There's shade from a tree branch

questions.q_zh: 猫在墙上做什么？
questions.q_en: What does the cat do on the wall?

options.pinyin: guānchá xiǎoqū lǐ fāshēng de yíqiè
options.text_en: Observes everything happening in the compound

title: The Changing Coffee Order
text_zh: 我在公司楼下的咖啡店工作了半年，注意到一个有趣的规律。有一个穿西装的男人每天早上八点十五分准时来，总是点同一杯咖啡：美式，中杯，不加糖。但是最近三个星期他的订单突然变了——先是换成了拿铁，然后改成了热巧克力，最后变成了一杯温热的牛奶。我忍不住观察他的变化：三周前他来的时候表情很紧张，手机一直响个不停。两周前他换了一身休闲装，脸上的表情放松了一些。上周他来的时候没有带公文包，坐在窗边慢慢地喝，看了半小时窗外的风景。今天他又来了，点了一杯美式——和最初一模一样。但他的眼神不同了，比以前平静很多。我猜他经历了什么重要的事情。一个人的咖啡订单也许比他自己的话更诚实——当你需要清醒的时候喝美式，需要安慰的时候喝甜的，找到答案以后又回到了原点。
text_pinyin: Wǒ zài gōngsī lóu xià de kāfēi diàn gōngzuò le bàn nián, zhùyì dào yí gè yǒuqù de guīlǜ. Yǒu yí gè chuān xīzhuāng de nánrén měi tiān zǎoshang bā diǎn shíwǔ fēn zhǔnshí lái, zǒng shì diǎn tóng yì bēi kāfēi: měishì, zhōng bēi, bù jiā táng. Dànshì zuìjìn sān gè xīngqī tā de dìngdān tūrán biàn le — xiān shì huàn chéng le ná tiě, ránhòu gǎi chéng le rè qiǎokèlì, zuìhòu biàn chéng le yì bēi wēnrè de niúnǎi. Wǒ rěn bu zhù guānchá tā de biànhuà: sān zhōu qián tā lái de shíhou biǎoqíng hěn jǐnzhāng, shǒujī yìzhí xiǎng gè bù tíng. Liǎng zhōu qián tā huàn le yì shēn xiūxián zhuāng, liǎn shàng de biǎoqíng fàngsōng le yìxiē. Shàng zhōu tā lái de shíhou méiyǒu dài gōngwén bāo, zuò zài chuāng biān mànmàn de hē, kàn le bàn xiǎoshí chuāng wài de fēngjǐng. Jīntiān tā yòu lái le, diǎn le yì bēi měishì — hé zuìchū yì mó yíyàng. Dàn tā de yǎnshén bùtóng le, bǐ yǐqián píngjìng hěn duō. Wǒ cāi tā jīnglì le shénme zhòngyào de shìqing. Yí gè rén de kāfēi dìngdān yěxǔ bǐ tā zìjǐ de huà gèng chéngshí — dāng nǐ xūyào qīngxǐng de shíhou hē měishì, xūyào ānwèi de shíhou hē tián de, zhǎo dào dá'àn yǐhòu yòu huí dào le yuándiǎn.
text_en: I've worked at the coffee shop below my company's building for half a year and noticed an interesting pattern. A man in a suit comes at exactly 8:15 every morning and always orders the same thing: Americano, medium, no sugar. But the past three weeks his order suddenly changed — first to a latte, then hot chocolate, finally a cup of warm milk. I couldn't help observing his changes: three weeks ago he looked tense, his phone ringing nonstop. Two weeks ago he came in casual clothes, his expression more relaxed. Last week he came without a briefcase, sat by the window and slowly drank, watching the scenery outside for half an hour. Today he came again and ordered an Americano — exactly the same as the beginning. But his eyes were different, much calmer than before. I suspect he went through something important. A person's coffee order might be more honest than their own words — when you need alertness you drink an Americano, when you need comfort you drink something sweet, and after finding your answer you return to where you started.
